So that's your transition from to your main body. Now the main body of your script to get over here, the most important thing for the main body is number one, okay, that it's not front-loaded, which means you don't go in with the listing benefit after benefit after benefit after benefit and you become an expert at your product and you start talking about all the features and blah blah blah blah blah and what happens is you end up preaching about your product and they're just listening and eventually they tune out. Okay, so your script should be short, the body should be no more than let's say a half to three-quarters of a page, okay, at which point when you're done with that you should have given them one or two very powerful distinctions about your product, okay, and make sure remember when we don't sell features, and this is basic influence 101, right, you don't sell features we sell benefits, people are not so concerned about, you know, every feature that a computer might have, what they want to know is that it will make their life easier, it will make it easier to surf the internet to find out the things they want, it allowed to have more time to spend with their family because they can be more productive, you get it so you don't sell features you sell benefits, okay, now in terms of the flow of a presentation it needs to flow seamlessly, in other words it has to flow in a way where there are certain chunks that sound great but then there are stop, I call them stopping off points which are these sections where you have transitions, so if you say a powerful statement, okay, and then you say another powerful statement, and then you say another powerful statement, eventually the three powerful statements start to lose their power, so the idea is after you say one powerful statement, you want to hammer something home, you say to the client, you follow me so far, and they say yes, you get it, so you want to say you follow me so far, make sense, are you with me, get the person to say yes, on two levels that's important, number one is so the person doesn't tune out because you're just preaching to them versus making it a dialogue, dialogue back and forth, not a monologue, right, and secondly because you want to get the prospect into the habit of saying yes, you don't want to be asking questions that might end in no because the person says no, no, no, no, no, then you want to get the yes out of them for your actual close is a bit more difficult, now understand this, you know, my technique, the straight line is far more advanced than simply thinking that we can get people to say yes five times to simple stuff and that will allow them to close, it's not like that, what I'm saying is, is you don't want to get somebody into the habit of saying no, okay, when you say does that make sense so far, they say yeah, it does, you're in rapport, if you say does that make sense, they say no, it doesn't, and you try to go forward, you're out of rapport, you break rapport, all they're saying so far, this guy doesn't care about what I'm saying, he's not even listening to me, you understand, so these little stopping off points where you actually say you're with me so long, make sense, and they say yes, great, boom, it keeps you in rapport, okay, next, the use of what I call colloquialisms, and these are, what it really means is speaking in layman's terms, you don't want to speak in a way that it sounds like it's canned and it's actually your script, you want to sound natural, like you're speaking to a friend, speaking to someone that you want to connect with emotionally and not just logically, now along with that, with colloquialism, remember, you still need to come off like an expert, so there's a balance to be struck, it's not all like, hey, we're like these dems do's and don'ts, like a Brooklyn guy who's not educated, right, you still want to sound educated like an expert, but don't try to talk above somebody and all these little buzz phrases that end up losing their power and the person tunes out, okay, so use colloquialisms and make it sound natural, but always remember that when you're influencing, you need to be perceived as the expert, okay, next point here, with scripts, the best way to master a script is to actually practice by reading the scripts out loud, and the way I do this, especially in the beginning when I'm developing a script and I get paid, let me tell you, big money to write scripts for companies, where I go in there and the scripts are awful, I go and I redo the whole thing, and it's a, for me, it's a painstaking lengthy process, but what I do is I'll write the first generation of a script, then I'll actually go out and start working with the sales force, in fact, there's some footage here that I'm going to cut to, which will show you me actually doing this in one of the companies I was working with, and you'll actually see me going with a script that wasn't quite perfect, and now I read back and forth to people and I listen to them and I hear what words are giving them trouble, meaning tongue twisters, what sentences aren't flowing correctly, what transitions are awkward, then I rewrite the script and go back and train with them again, until I make sure that every single word in the script flows seamlessly, and anybody, not just the top, top salesman there, can say it well, anyone can say it well, okay, so for that, you know, to that end, what it's really, I mean, one of the key elements is what I call balance, in other words, the script has to be balanced with the number of syllables, number of beats in each sentence, okay, if something just because it rambles on and rambles on, the human ear is very attuned to this stuff and it tunes out, so you have to make sure that, you know, it's pleasing to the ear, there's right number of syllables, okay, and remember that you're never not closing, you're always closing, okay, not necessarily the sale itself, but you're closing on that, the person should listen to the next paragraph or even the next word that you say, okay, and again, the first five words that you say, and this is, I said earlier that if you've, I'm sure you've been through the actual straight line course, where you've learned that we, as human beings, we judge books by their covers, we just, we would not do what we all do, and even the people who taught us not to judge the books by their covers, like our parents, right, because it's human nature to do that, and really in the first four seconds, the people that you're trying to influence are already making a mental picture of, picture of you, so they're gonna either judge you as being, you know, that expert, that enthusiastic, sharpest attack person, or they're gonna find you dull and boring and not enthusiastic, at which point to say, ah, this person's not worth listening to, okay, so it's very important that those first words, like, you know, you say to someone, it's simple as, watch, I say, you know, hi, it's Pam there, or hi, it's Pam there, same words, but the second one comes out, upbeat, enthusiastic, sharpest attack, now, of course, Pam is not gonna buy, because I say, hi, it's Pam there, but if I say, hi, it's Pam there, she's like, ugh, another dull intrusion into my already dull day, versus like, oh, well, if I say, oh, it's Pam there, she's like, hmm, what's that? Someone's like enthusiastic, it just sort of breaks somebody's state, and you essentially elevate somebody to a higher state. On some level, influence itself is about, it's the transference of emotion, the emotion of certainty. You're going to encounter where you're very certain about your product or your service or your coaching, whatever it is that you're doing, and the person's not that certain, so you're transferring that emotion of certainty to somebody else, okay, so it's very important that you learn to, you know, master those tonal patterns, and especially in the beginning, to come off sharpest attack, enthusiastic as hell, and an expert in your field. Next, with your scripts, it's important that you're honest and ethical, okay, and this is very easy to do, because just when you're writing the script, you ask yourself with every single sentence, you say to yourself, am I coming from a place of integrity, am I coming from a place of ethics, or am I starting to exaggerate and sort of mislead, see this, I'll tell you, so I've written scripts just so you know, back in the early days when I was discovering the straight line, and I wrote some scripts that were accurate, but they weren't ethical, they weren't lies, but they were some misstatements or really omissions of fact, so make sure that you know, for your own ethical, you know, your own integrity, keep your scripts accurate, don't include any lies or wild exaggerations, and by the way, just so you know, if you're a sales manager or an owner, an entrepreneur looking to use the script building technology, if you put a script out into your sales force and it's riddled with lies or exaggerations, they know it, and if every day your salespeople are out there hammering away on the phone or in the field, and they're telling lies, it starts to seep into your entire corporate culture, and next thing you know, you know, the people will be trying to rob and steal from you, your own employees will steal from you because they think you're a thief, because you're telling them to go out and lie to people, so this idea that you can have a script that's exaggerated and full of deceit, not the thing that's going to permeate to your entire corporate culture is foolish, so the scripts need to be accurate, they need to be compliant, and they need to really reflect your corporate culture, which is one of ethics and integrity, okay, so the question I get asked is, you know, how, you know, when should I use my script? And the answer is always, you should always use a script whether you're in person or on the phone, so you say, well how do I use a script if I'm in person? Very simple, you haven't memorized, you want to know the script so well that you're moved past the point of the actual words, remember, 9% of human communication is words, the other 91% is tonality and body language, by memorizing the script, knowing the words themselves, it allows you to do that automatically so the rest of your brain can be focusing on your tonality, on your body language, and also looking at the person and trying to gather the intelligence to listen to them closely, not being so caught up in your own head, if you're so caught up in your own words and trying to like say them, you don't know them by heart, well your brain is only someone, your conscious brain is only so much processing power and you'll find you'll be missing those vital cues that you can get from the other people, okay? In terms of how you tailor your scripts, what we typically do is you'll see as you start writing more and more scripts, you can pull various language patterns from other scripts and you can make them into a cohesive pattern and to that end I'm giving you, in the script building technology here, is some sample scripts that you can actually model and you know, modeling is going to just save you a tremendous amount of time to learn because again my, you know, success has come from trial and error and from, you know, 25 years of writing or plus of writing scripts, you know, almost in a, you know, in a way where I'm a zealot of it, I torture myself with every single word, every single paragraph, every single transition, so learn from what I've done, model my scripts, translate, you know, take them and use them into your own industry and you'll have this all laid out in the manual itself, I just want to give you a sort of, you know, visually so you understand this, okay? Now in terms of language patterns here, we very often talk about, you know, the use of effective language patterns and really what are language patterns and, you know, how do you use them to close the deal and essentially what a language pattern is, it's a chunk of words, maybe a paragraph or two paragraphs that has a defined beginning and a defined end with a specific outcome attached to it. You can have a language pattern that's specifically designed to increase the certainty of your product, you can have a language pattern that is designed to sell your company, one that's designed to sell yourself, right? So my, when I go to a company and I train the sales forces, but really 80% of what I do, very, only 20% is actual seminars, 80% is corporate work, what I do is I will actually have the salespeople work and create five language patterns, five chunks that they can say that are intelligent, that are ethical and that are persuasive and that are sexy, five about themselves, five language patterns about the company that they work for and actually five language patterns that are about the product specifically that help them sell the product. And remember, again, we have, just want to go through this, what I mean by that is remember, we have the three 10s, we have the three 10s, right? And that's product, you and your company. And what we're doing is the language patterns that we're running here for each are designed. If we have one here and 10 here, this is our continuum of certainty. Our goal is to get people to absolute certainty, which is a 10 for all three of these things. That's what your language patterns are doing and they're occurring along the straight line. So in the open, this is the close, this is the open, in the open, they might be at a three on your product, a two at you and a one about your company. And as they go down the straight line, you keep increasing this, increasing, they're moving down this continuum, increasing, increasing, boom, until you get to a 10, 10, 10. And then of course, there are language patterns that lower someone's action threshold, their action threshold, right? And we have other language patterns that help elevate the pain threshold. And of course, I caution you that when you elevate somebody's pain and want you to do it in a way that's empathetic, sympathetic, you're not there to make someone hurt, you're there to let someone realize that while they might think that not taking actions in their best interest, well, in the real world where they sit right now, if they don't take action, they're going to be feeling a lot of pain because they're already feeling pain right now. And if they don't make any decisions, don't make any, you know, new, take any new actions, well, nothing's going to change. So that is ethical to help someone insert into the pain of not taking action, but it has to be balanced with giving them a solution as well. That, you know, you're going to guide that you can help them, you can, you know, get them to where they need to be in life, the goals, their vision, okay, and move them closer. Now, let me just flip the page here. Okay. And lastly, of those, you'll be at the introduction, the body of the script. And of course, lastly, we have the, the close.
